mnemonics: P-Hacking is Peeking: Repeatedly checking data for significance until you find a pattern by chance, like peeking during a test.
Null Hypothesis (H₀) is the 'No' Hypothesis: It assumes there is no effect or no difference, the default position to be tested against.
Type I Error is a 'False Alarm': Rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true (e.g., concluding a new drug works when it doesn't).
Type II Error is a 'Miss': Failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is actually false (e.g., concluding a new drug doesn't work when it does).
keyPoints: A statistical test assesses evidence provided by data to decide whether to reject a null hypothesis (H₀) in favor of an alternative hypothesis (Hₐ).

The p-value is the probability of observing results as extreme as, or more extreme than, the results actually observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true. A low p-value (typically < 0.05) provides evidence against H₀.
Statistical power is the probability that a test correctly rejects a false null hypothesis (1 - probability of a Type II error). It increases with larger effect sizes and larger sample sizes.
commonMistakes: Interpreting a high p-value as evidence *for* the null hypothesis. A high p-value only indicates that the data is not inconsistent with H₀; it does not prove H₀ is true.
Confusing statistical significance with practical significance. A result can be statistically significant (low p-value) but have such a small effect size that it is meaningless in the real world.
Data dredging (p-hacking): Performing multiple tests on a dataset without correction, increasing the family-wise error rate and the chance of a false positive (Type I error).
